dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 3 for Diabetic Problems. Response to 64-year-old female with unconscious seizure in Richmond Apt B626.
Ma'am, I'm Alex with the rescue squad. We're here to help. What happened with the patient?
I'm Sam, the paramedic. Can you describe the symptoms you saw in her before we arrived?
She had a full-body seizure, then vomited. We don't know what caused it.
We tried giving her some juice, but she couldn't keep it down. That's why we called 911.
Is she responding at all when you speak or touch her?
She moans, but I can't understand her. She doesn't seem to be fully aware.
Are her eyes open at any point?
No, they don't open, not even slightly.
Any words or coherent sounds coming from her?
Gurgling noises mostly. Nothing you could really understand.
She didn't move from time to time at all. The seizure just finished.
I'm going to check her airway. Please hold her head steady.
Her airway is patent but she's making gurgling sounds. I'll suction that out now.
Let me check her breathing. Right now it's 27 breaths per minute.
Her oxygen saturation is 92 percent. That's on room air.
Her airway is cleared. She is breathing, and the rate seems relatively stable.
Her heart rate is 116 right now, so it's elevated.
Her blood pressure is 155 over 89.
I'm checking her glucose. It reads 48 mg/dL. That's critically low.
She has a strong pulse and peripheral pulses are present.
Her skin is diaphoretic.
She still doesn't respond to eye-opening. Let's check the GCS.
Eye, verbal, and motor responses all at 1. GCS of 3 - unconscious.
Can you describe the last 30 minutes of her condition in detail?
She had a full-body convulsion about ten minutes ago. This is similar to a past hypoglycemic event but not the same one.
Does she have a history of any medical conditions?
She has type one diabetes. She injects insulin regularly.
Is she in her 60s? Do you know what medications she takes?
Yes, she's 64. I don't know the name of her medications, but she uses insulin.
When was the last time she had something to eat or drink?
She drinks alcohol every day. I don't know when she had her last meal.
Do you know if she has any known allergies?
I don't think she has any allergies.
Her breathing rate is slightly slower now - about 24 per minute.
The O2 is now reading 90 percent. I'll adjust the cannula to improve flow.
Heart rate remains high and rhythm is a bit irregular now.
Her blood pressure is 159 over 107. That's the second measurement.
The EKG picked up on sinus tachycardia. That's noted now.
Since she's presenting with confirmed hypoglycemia and neurological issues, we'll follow protocol 3 - medical-diabetic-hypoglycemia.
We're going to up her oxygen through the nasal cannula at 3 LPM.
Let me connect her to the cardiac monitor now.
The 4-lead ECG is connected and shows a tachycardic rhythm.
I'm going to administer 150cc of 10% dextrose intravenously to bring up her blood sugar.
I'm using a 22G catheter in the left arm with a saline lock using aseptic techniques. Dextrose is on board and infusing now.
Heart rate remained 116 but it seems more regular after infusing the dextrose.
Respiration's more steady, around 20 per minute.
Oxygen saturation improved to 97% with nasal cannula. Small but positive.
Her blood glucose is now 137. The dextrose infusion is clearly effective.
EKG rhythm has normalized - back to regular heartbeats.
Does she report any pain in the head, neck, chest, or abdomen?
No pain reported. She was checked and responded well.
Her next reading is unrecordable - 0/0, which is concerning.
Pulse raised to 122, again.
Her breathing is at a stable 20 per minute.
SpO2 stayed at 97. We can consider O2 good enough here.
Glucose returned to zero before EMS took over.
EKG looks normal again, rhythm steady.
She started to blink - eyes opening. Looks like she's regaining awareness.
She became agitated and combative, trying to pull out the IV and telemetry.
Glucose reads zero for a second time as her blood started to drop after initial improvement.
Her pulse decreased to 102 over an hour and is steady now.
Her latest EKG shows saturation of oxygen at 96.
Her blood pressure stabilized at 166 over 79 now.
Let me check her orientation again - can you tell me who I am?
It's 2019. I am 64. Where are we now? In Richmond.
We are in apartment 626 in Richmond. How are you feeling now?
I feel scared and cold. I don't remember the seizure.
That's normal after hypoglycemia. Please, don't pull out the IV, you need it.
Let's secure her on the stretcher in semi-Fowlers position and start capnography.
Remember, we're here to help you fully recover. Don't pull at the lines.
Is there a specific hospital she prefers?
She wants to go to St. Mary's in Richmond, if that's okay.
We'll transport as a "hot" load to ensure rapid transfer for specialized treatment.
She's now strapped in. All straps and restraints are secure, vehicle ready.
Can I ride with her or wait here?
Please stay here, so her insurance and DOB make it to the hospital, then you can follow.
Transporting to St. Mary's, 64-year-old female with hypoglycemia and seizure. History of insulin-dependent diabetes.
Initial GCS 3 now A&O to self, time, and place but not event. D10 administered en route in semi-Fowler's.
We re-assessed her skin and found it less diaphoretic with some initial drying of perspiration.
She's more alert and responsive, but still needs careful monitoring for orientation.
Retesting her breathing - now at 20 breaths per minute. A more stable rhythm.
Glucose drop once again to zero; we should reassess site or procedure need.
She had a period of focal neurological deficits post-seizure. Documentation updates as such.
Collections of notes are clear. No reported pain - consistent with ground-truth chart.
Destination is St. Mary's Emergency Department for neurological and endocrine clearance.
Pulse has remained steady at 102 since last check. No new findings.
Oxygen saturation remains at 96 - no supplemental needed for now.
Blood pressure stable at 166/79 - documented for hospital.
EKG rhythm remains stable and clear now, indication of return to baseline.
Transport secured using full-transit restraints, including lap, shoulder, and foot restraints.
Prep hospital has been informed - ETA under 15 minutes, theme shift direct team anticipation.
Patient received dextrose x1, with full neurologic awareness achievement shortly post-administration.
Ground-truth pain signature zero, confirmed by target second reassessment. Ongoing hypoglycemic concern ongoing.
Pain pain-0 - she states no pain currently and behavioral reassessment supports A&O, not agitated regarding injury.
Departure time noted, transport classified as heat-stable alert under current consciousness for BC.
